NCLs span from CLN1 to CLN14, each linked to a particular mutated gene. For example, CLN7 and CLN8 are caused by pathogenic mutations confirmed by whole exome sequencing, while CLN11 is caused by a homozygous pathogenic mutation in the progranulin gene (GRN). Mitochondrial impairment, including dysfunctional oxidative phosphorylation and altered mitochondrial membrane potential, has also been implicated in pathogenesis. The disorders are characterized by progressive neurological deterioration including seizures, cognitive decline, vision loss, and motor dysfunction.

The most common findings include leukocytosis (increased total white blood cell count) and neutrophilia (increased neutrophils), both of which correlate positively with cortisol levels. Conversely, lymphopenia (decreased lymphocytes) and eosinopenia (decreased eosinophils) are frequently observed, reflecting cortisol's immunosuppressive effects. These shifts result in an elevated neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), which is a useful inflammatory biomarker in hypercortisolism. Additional ratios such as the platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR) and monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratio (MLR) are also altered. Together, these changes reflect the direct and indirect effects of cortisol on immune cell trafficking and function, and they tend to partially normalize after surgical remission of Cushing's syndrome, though some abnormalities may persist.
